config.title: Vietnam, three rhythms
config.destination: Hanoi · Hoi An · Ho Chi Minh City
config.coverNote: I built this as a value-conscious first trip through Vietnam: enough history to understand the country, enough food to make each city memorable, and enough open time that it never feels like a checklist. Nothing here is represented as booked. The 2027 dates and rates are a transparent planning proxy for the 2028 trip you requested, and I will reprice every component before anything is reserved.
config.driverPhrase: Làm ơn đưa tôi đến đây.

days.title: Fly to Vietnam
days.summary: Depart IAD at 11:50 AM on the live planning itinerary.

days.title: Arrive in Hanoi
days.summary: Private transfer, check in, and keep the first evening deliberately light.

days.title: Old Quarter and food
days.summary: A guided walk through Hanoi's historic core with a serious street-food introduction.

days.title: Ideas and memory
days.summary: Temple of Literature, Hoa Lo, and the Vietnamese Women's Museum.

days.title: Art and living culture
days.summary: Fine Arts Museum, a focused architecture or craft walk, and an open evening.

days.title: Hanoi to Hoi An
days.summary: Fly to Da Nang, transfer to Hoi An, and take a first slow loop through Ancient Town.

days.title: My Son before the buses
days.summary: Early private guide to the Cham sanctuary, with the afternoon protected.

days.title: Market to table
days.summary: Morning market and cooking experience, then pool, tailor, spa, or café time.

days.title: Choose your pace
days.summary: Countryside and Tra Que, or a completely unscheduled day.

days.title: Hoi An to Saigon
days.summary: Fly to Ho Chi Minh City, settle in, then a rooftop drink and easy dinner.

days.title: The twentieth century
days.summary: Reunification Palace and War Remnants Museum with contextual guidance.

days.title: Chợ Lớn and street food
days.summary: Chinatown temples and markets, then an evening tasting route.

days.title: Art, design, and dinner
days.summary: Fine Arts Museum, independent galleries, and a chef-led dinner.

days.title: One open day
days.summary: Optional Cu Chi, optional Mekong, or stay in the city for a slow final day.

days.title: Begin the trip home
days.summary: Late checkout if available, fly Saigon to Hanoi, then connect to IAD.

days.title: Arrive home
days.summary: Land at IAD at 9:50 AM on the live planning itinerary.

itinerary.title: IAD to Hanoi via Seoul
itinerary.subtitle: Korean Air / Delta planning itinerary · $1,306 per adult round trip
itinerary.personalNote: I chose IAD because it keeps the long-haul on a practical one-ticket itinerary.

itinerary.title: Hanoi Tirant Hotel
itinerary.subtitle: $102 per night live display · 4 nights
itinerary.personalNote: My balanced Hanoi pick: central, polished, and good value without paying for resort facilities in a city stay.

itinerary.title: Private Old Quarter food walk
itinerary.personalNote: This is one of the two experiences I would lock early. A strong guide matters more than collecting a long restaurant list.

itinerary.title: Hanoi to Da Nang
itinerary.subtitle: Nonstop domestic flight · exact schedule to be selected

details.Planning allowance: Included in $360 to $520 domestic-flight range for two with checked bags

itinerary.title: La Siesta Hoi An Resort & Spa
itinerary.subtitle: $111 per night live display · 4 nights
itinerary.personalNote: This is where I would take the resort upgrade. It changes the middle of the trip without distorting the budget.

itinerary.title: Private early visit to My Son Sanctuary
itinerary.personalNote: Go early, before the larger tour groups and the strongest heat.

itinerary.title: Hoi An market and cooking experience
itinerary.personalNote: I would choose a small class that treats the market as part of the lesson, not a photo stop.

itinerary.title: Da Nang to Ho Chi Minh City
itinerary.subtitle: Nonstop domestic flight · exact schedule to be selected

itinerary.title: Mai House Saigon Hotel
itinerary.subtitle: $144 per night live display · 5 nights
itinerary.personalNote: A proper five-star finish without trophy-hotel pricing.

itinerary.title: Ho Chi Minh City Museum of Fine Arts
itinerary.locationName: Ho Chi Minh City Museum of Fine Arts

itinerary.title: Ho Chi Minh City to Hanoi
itinerary.subtitle: Same-day positioning flight for the international connection
itinerary.personalNote: I would protect a long same-airport buffer in Hanoi and book this on a dependable schedule.

picks.name: Temple of Literature
picks.description: Vietnam's first national university and one of Hanoi's clearest windows into Confucian scholarly culture.
picks.note: Go with a guide or read before you arrive. The symbolism rewards context.

picks.name: Vietnamese Women's Museum
picks.description: An unusually strong social-history museum centered on women's lives, family, work, and conflict.
picks.note: I would not skip this. It adds human texture that the larger state sites cannot.

picks.name: Chả Cá Thăng Long
picks.description: Turmeric-marinated fish cooked with dill and scallions at the table.
picks.note: Order the house specialty and arrive hungry.

picks.name: Hoi An Ancient Town
picks.description: A preserved trading-port core of merchant houses, assembly halls, temples, and river streets.
picks.note: Walk it once early and once after dark. The mood changes completely.

picks.name: Morning Glory Original
picks.description: A polished introduction to central Vietnamese regional dishes.
picks.note: Useful for the first night when you want range without a complicated decision.

picks.name: Madam Khanh, the Bánh Mì Queen
picks.description: A famous, fast-moving bánh mì stop near the Ancient Town.
picks.note: Treat it as a snack or light lunch, not a ceremonial meal.

picks.name: Nữ Eatery
picks.description: Small, modern Vietnamese plates close to the Japanese Covered Bridge.
picks.note: A good counterpoint to the traditional restaurants on the route.

picks.name: Reunification Palace
picks.description: A preserved modernist government complex tied directly to the end of the Vietnam War.
picks.note: The rooms make more sense with a guide who can explain the building as well as the politics.

picks.name: War Remnants Museum
picks.description: A powerful and intentionally Vietnamese presentation of wartime experience.
picks.note: Give it time, then leave space afterward. This is not a quick photo stop.

picks.name: Ho Chi Minh City Museum of Fine Arts
picks.description: Vietnamese art in a striking colonial-era building near the center.
picks.note: Pair it with contemporary gallery stops so the art story does not end in the museum.

picks.name: Ănăn Saigon
picks.description: Contemporary Vietnamese cooking above a traditional market district.
picks.note: This is one of the two dinners I would reserve before the trip.

picks.name: The Workshop Coffee
picks.description: A specialty-coffee room above the city center's street noise.
picks.note: Use it as a deliberate mid-afternoon reset.

contacts.name: Gabe
contacts.note: Text me first. If the plan needs to move, I will help move it.

guideBlocks.heading: What is and is not booked
guideBlocks.body: This is a researched planning itinerary. Every flight, room, guide, transfer, ticket, restaurant, price, and cancellation policy remains pending until you approve it and I send a booking confirmation.

guideBlocks.heading: Why IAD, not DCA
guideBlocks.body: IAD offers the practical one-ticket transpacific options. Starting at DCA would add a domestic positioning leg and more failure points without improving the long-haul fare enough to justify it.

guideBlocks.heading: 2028 pricing method
guideBlocks.body: 2028 airfare is not yet in the normal public sales window. I priced exact February 20 to March 6, 2027 inventory as a same-season proxy. The hub shows the planning floor, not a bookable 2028 quote.

guideBlocks.heading: Getting around
guideBlocks.body: Use private airport transfers on arrival days. In Hanoi and Saigon, Grab is the simplest everyday option. In Hoi An, walk the Ancient Town and use a car for My Son, the countryside, and Da Nang airport.

guideBlocks.heading: Power and outlets
guideBlocks.body: Vietnam uses 220V. Outlets commonly accept Type A, C, and sometimes F plugs. Phones and laptops are usually dual voltage, but check hair tools. Pack one compact universal adapter per traveler and keep a charging brick in your carry-on.

guideBlocks.heading: Food pacing
guideBlocks.body: I would prebook one serious food guide in Hanoi, one cooking experience in Hoi An, and two destination dinners in Saigon. The rest should stay flexible enough for a guide or hotel to make a current local call.
